coredata.dc:title: Carbon nano dots mixed rice bran oil as a cutting fluid for enhanced lubrication/cooling in milling of additively manufactured 316 stainless steel
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ross, Nimel Sworna
Mashinini, Peter Madindwa
Rai, Ritu
Gupta, Munish Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Nanofluids, which have improved thermal conductivity compared to regular lubricants, can assist in the dissipation of heat while cutting materials. A colloidal suspension can be formed by dispersing nanoparticles within a base fluid. Their high thermal conductivity and viscosity make them a promising heat transfer fluid option; by using nano-additives, their cooling and lubricating capabilities may be further improved. Recently, carbon nanodots have grabbed the attention due to their many desirable characteristics, including their high solubility, chemical inertness, and low toxicity. Therefore, in this perspective, the thermophysical properties of rice bran oil enriched with carbon nanodots and their considerable impact on the machinability of 316 stainless steel produced additively has been examined. The different wt.% (0–1.25) of carbon nanodots are dispersed in rice bran oil to test the wettability, thermal conductivity, and dynamic viscosity. After that, milling tests are conducted under dry, flood, minimum quantity lubrication, and nano-minimum quantity lubrication conditions. The findings admit that the inclusion of nanoparticles in rice bran oil at 1 wt.% results in lower contact angle, higher thermal conductivity, and higher viscosity. In addition, the employment of nanoparticles in minimum quantity lubrication reduced the roughness by 60–63 %, 40–43 %, and 22–25 % in relation to dry, flood, and MQL conditions, respectively. The reduction in roughness has a direct influence on tool wear.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Thermal conductivity
Viscosity
Thermophysical properties

Influence of CND based NPs in base oil

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Types, characteristics, mixing methods, and base oils for Nanofluids.
(a) Preparation of Nanoparticles (b) TEM and XRD of CND.
Mechanism of NPs amid tool-W/p contact.
Contact angle variation of NF at distinct concentrations.
Surface roughness at distinct speed-feed combinations.
3D Surface profiles at Vc = 60 m/min and fr = 0.12 mm/rev under distinct cutting strategies.
Flank wear at distinct speed-feed combinations.
Wear types at Vc = 60 m/min and fr = 0.12 mm/rev under distinct cutting strategies.
Parameters and specifications used in this work.

head.ce:title: Carbon nano dots mixed rice bran oil as a cutting fluid for enhanced lubrication/cooling in milling of additively manufactured 316 stainless steel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Graphic Era (Deemed to be university)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluids, which have improved thermal conductivity compared to regular lubricants, can assist in the dissipation of heat while cutting materials. A colloidal suspension can be formed by dispersing nanoparticles within a base fluid. Their high thermal conductivity and viscosity make them a promising heat transfer fluid option; by using nano-additives, their cooling and lubricating capabilities may be further improved. Recently, carbon nanodots have grabbed the attention due to their many desirable characteristics, including their high solubility, chemical inertness, and low toxicity. Therefore, in this perspective, the thermophysical properties of rice bran oil enriched with carbon nanodots and their considerable impact on the machinability of 316 stainless steel produced additively has been examined. The different wt.% (0–1.25) of carbon nanodots are dispersed in rice bran oil to test the wettability, thermal conductivity, and dynamic viscosity. After that, milling tests are conducted under dry, flood, minimum quantity lubrication, and nano-minimum quantity lubrication conditions. The findings admit that the inclusion of nanoparticles in rice bran oil at 1 wt.% results in lower contact angle, higher thermal conductivity, and higher viscosity. In addition, the employment of nanoparticles in minimum quantity lubrication reduced the roughness by 60–63 %, 40–43 %, and 22–25 % in relation to dry, flood, and MQL conditions, respectively. The reduction in roughness has a direct influence on tool wear.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the twelve disruptive technologies that make up the fourth industrial revolution is additive manufacturing (AM), often known as 3D printing [1]. In comparison to polymers and ceramics, the metals AM sector has evolved at a significantly quicker rate in recent years. Due to its ability to produce objects in almost-final shape, AM has recently become a popular technology in space applications with only minor surface machining and reduced buy-to-fly ratios [2]. Metallic materials are being employed in selective laser melting (SLM) and directed-energy deposition (DED), two common types of AM technologies [3]. Although SLM is time-consuming and has a restriction on the size of the components that may be created due to the chamber size of the machines, it can manufacture items with great dimensional precision and outstanding mechanical qualities. DED is faster and has no size restrictions, but the resulting pieces are less precise and have inferior mechanical qualities [4]. Laser-based powder bed fusion (LPBF), a SLM process produces components from powdered raw material by fusing certain volumes of elements layer by layer. This manufacturing concept allows users to create highly customized parts, and personalize products for specific uses [5]. Recent research has focused on understanding the physical principles of LPBF and how process management affects component qualities [6]. LPBF-created components not have adequate surface quality or dimensional accuracy for direct application. Thus, functional surfaces of LPBF-manufactured components frequently require subtractive processes to increase the part quality [7]. Therefore, a hybrid method that combines the two processes might result in the production of a wide variety of goods, the goal of which would be to make the most of the benefits that each technique has to offer [8]. The part's features have a direct impact on the component's surface quality and reliability [9]. Machining produces good functioning surfaces on cast or wrought components. LPBF materials differ from cast or wrought components because of substantial temperature gradients and complex heat transport during cyclic processing [10]. Despite having the same chemical content as wrought or cast metals, parts made with LPBF may be quite different in terms of machinability.

Cutting fluids (CFs) are a commonly employed lubrication/cooling (L/C) strategy in machining operations [11,12]. Depending on the material to be machined and production circumstances, these CFs are occasionally loaded with some additives [13]. Although CFs may enhance the machining performance, but they may have detrimental environmental and human health consequences [14,15]. Minimum quantity lubrication (MQL) systems were created to reduce the negative effects of CF by using air and a very small volume of coolant throughout the cutting progression [16]. MQL may lessen the temperature of the contact region at the chip-tool junction due tothe increased lubricant penetration made possible by the pressurized air [17,18]. Ramoni et al. explored the effect of MQL with edible oil in the milling of AMed metal and compared that with dry and flood coolant strategies. The MQL enhanced the surface characteristic by 45–63 % in contrast to dry conditions and 23–43 % concerning flood cooling strategies [19]. Bedi et al. evaluated the influence of distinct vegetable oils (VOs) in MQL on the machinability of conventional AISI 304. Rice bran oil (RBO) in MQL exhibits a lower temperature, lesser cutting force, and better finished surface in relation to dry and coconut oil in MQL [20]. Potential MQL cutting of stainless steel (SS) with a coated cutter was studied by Dureja et al. All tests used straight-cutting oil (non-edible) with outstanding heat removal properties and superior wettability. In order to reduce flank wear, the MQL flow rate of 100 ml/hwas used [21].

The use of cutting oil with nanoparticles (NPs) to enhance the efficiency and tribological features of MQL cooling systems is becoming more popular. The most crucial element in the heat transfer function is the heat transfer coefficient (HTC) of nanofluids (NFs). Additionally, the NFs' efficient thermophysical characteristics have an impact on the HTC. Thermophysical qualities include thermal conductivity, which measures how well heat is transferred, dynamic viscosity, which influences pumping power and pressure loss, and specific heat, which shows how well the NF can store and transmit heat away from the hot source [22]. NPs most commonly employed in various studies in different forms are metals like Cu, Ag, Ni, and Au; metal oxides like CuO, TiO2, Fe2O3, SiO2, and Al2O3; carbon materials like graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNT), Multi-walled CNT and diamond [23]. NFs may also be provided with surfactants to boost their stability, enhance their physical characteristics, and render them highly hydrophilic [24]. Sirin et al. compared the outcomes attained with dry, pure MQL and hBN NPs with oil. Although dry cutting provides poor surface smoothness, it can be observed that pure MQL and NPs + oil have significantly improved the situation. Surface roughness was decreased by roughly 39 and 47.2 %, respectively, using pure MQL and hBN mixed oil in contrast to the dry machining strategy [25]. Ross et al. investigated the evaluation of NF and its substantial effectiveness in the machining operation of analloy that had been conventionally prepared. Comparative analysis is done between MWCNT suspended in VO (edible oil) and various cooling environments. Utilizing NPs results in better surface smoothness and less tool wear [26]. Tiwari et al. studied the thermophysical properties of coconut oil (edible oil) with nano-Al2O3 and their considerable impact on the cutting of AISI-1040 steel. To study dynamic viscosity, wettability, and thermal conductivity, various percent weight concentrations of nano-Al2O3 are spread in coconut oil. These concentration ranges from 0.25 to 1.50 percent. The base fluid's dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity are considerably enhanced by the addition of NPs, which diminishes tool wear and elevates surface characteristics [27]. The basic characteristics of NFs in machining are displayed in Fig. 1 .

The literature and previous works depicted that the combination of Carbon nanodots (CND) and RBO as a CF has not yet been reported in the machining of different materials. Compared to several other CFs, RBO has comparatively high flash point. This makes it suited for high-speed machining and processes requiring substantial heat generation since it can sustain greater temperatures without burning or breaking. This encourages to investigate the potential benefits of using CND suspended in RBO for machining. This study examined the wettability, thermal conductivity, and dynamic viscosity of NFs at varied wt.% concentrations and the machining performance on additively manufactured AISI 316L SS. The advancement of tool wear as well as surface topography were evaluated as a part of the machining performance assessment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations and thermophysical properties measurement of CND based nano cutting fluids

ce:section.ce:para: In this work, CND and the two-step mixing process were used. CND, which have lateral widths of about 10 nm with lattice spacing of 0.21 and 0.34 nm, which correspond to the (100) and (002) graphite lattice planes [28]. Graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots and carbonized polymer dots are the three types of CND that are most frequently used, as shown in Fig. 2 . While CND are made up of both sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon networks, GQDs exclusively contain a sp2 hybridized carbon framework [29]. Furthermore, their surfaces can be easily functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, amino, carboxyl, and epoxy groups, providing additional advantages for interacting with both inorganic and organic moieties.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the entire preparation procedure (step-by-step). Initially, the hydrothermal method was used to create CND through green synthesis from Hibiscus sabdariffa. A certain quantity of green leaves was cleaned, powdered, and extracted using 150 ml of water. The resultant extract underwent an 8-hour autoclave at 160 °C. The liquid was centrifuged to remove bigger particles when it had cooled. To create CND, the liquid was later freeze-dried [31]. Fig. 3 (b) displays the Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) picture and X-ray diffraction (XRD) outcome.

RBO is a promising replacement for synthetic-based CFs that are sustainable, biodegradable, and friendly to the environment. The NFs (CND + RBO) were developed with a 0.25 wt.% increment of CND ranging from 0.0 to 1.25 wt.%. The selection of wt.% was based on the literature by Tiwari et al. [27]. In this investigation, the surfactant employed was sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate because of its high stability. Concerning previous studies, this research endeavor used a surfactant rate of 0.02 wt%. Green synthesized CND and RBO were mixed, then sonicated (using a Qsonica Sonicator) for around 30 min to create NFs. To obtain a homogeneous suspension without any phase separation, the sonication procedure was carried out continuously at a frequency of 40 Hz. The mixture was subjected to magnetic stirring using a Relitech magnetic stirrer for approximately 1h to achieve thorough homogenization. Fig. 4 provides a schematic illustration of how CND + RBO is prepared.

Prior to commencing the machining operation, an examination of the thermophysical characteristics of NF was carried out. The wetting capability of RBO with different concentrations of NF was investigated using a contact angle goniometer. A droplet of NF was dispensed onto a substrate at room temperature using a micrometer syringe, and the contact angle of the NF was measured. To assess NF characteristics, the Thermal Property Analyzer (TEMPOS) and rheometer (NETZSCH) were employed. TEMPOS measures thermal conductivity using the transient hot wire method. In the transient hot wire method, a wire with known resistance is placed in the NF, and the resulting temperature rise determines thermal conductivity. On the other hand, the rheometer applies controlled shear stress or strain to the NF, evaluating dynamic viscosity. The pressure required for extrusion is used to determine dynamic viscosity. These instruments play a crucial role in understanding and optimizing the performance of NF.

The 316L SS samples with a size of 70 × 50 × 10 mm3 was manufactured using the LPBF process (Fig. 5 ). The AMed method was performed utilizing a SLM 280 with a build volume of 280 × 280 × 365 mm and a laser scanning speed of 10 m/s, which utilized a ytterbium fiber laser. The LPBF process was used to produce the workpieces (W/ps), using metal powder with a size range of 30–60 µm that have a smooth, spherical surface. A layer thickness of 30 µm was used to create the specimens. Under the environment of argon, the melting of each layer was accomplished with laser scanning in a straight line. The following parameters were utilized: 40 W of laser power; a scanning velocity of 400 mm/s; 100 μm parallel scanning. The initial Ra value of the specimen before machining was 7.25 µm for a layer thickness of 30 µm, which is not suitable for many applications due to its poor surface roughness. The material was produced using the gas atomization process, and a laser beam with a diameter of 65 ± 5 µm.

The CVM COSMOS 850 was used for the milling process. To cut the LPBF processed 316L SS, a tungsten carbide insert with a TiAlN coating was utilized with the appropriate tool holder. The LPBF-created 316L SS finds its application in aerospace, medical implants, jigs and fixtures, moulds, and so on. For this machinability investigation, the W/p with dimensions of 70 × 50 × 10 mm3 was taken into consideration. The other parameters and specifications are presented in Table 1 . The cutting parameters were taken into account with the help of the manufacturer’s guidelines and published research works.

Dry conditions refer to the absence of CFs or lubricants during the machining operation. Dry cutting simplifies the machining process by eliminating the need for fluid supply systems, coolant maintenance, and related equipment. This can streamline operations and reduce setup and maintenance time. Flood conditions refer to the use of a coolant or cutting fluid that is continuously supplied and circulated to the cutting zone during the machining operation. It involves flooding the cutting area with the CF, which helps in various aspects of the machining process. Flood coolant acts as a lubricant amid the chip-tool-W/p junction, reducing heat and friction at the cutting region. Compared to certain synthetic coolants, RBO is an environmentally preferable alternative because it is a natural and biodegradable oil. It can be an attractive option for applications where sustainability and reduced environmental impact are priorities. Polar elements including S, O, N, and P, as well as polar groups like –COOR, –COR, –OH, –COOH, –CHO, –NH2, –NHCH3, –NH3, and –NROH, are found in vegetable oils. Van der Waals forces cause these polar atoms or groups to have a significant affinity for the W/p surface and create the physical adsorption layer. Friction can be decreased using the physical adsorption film. At 38 °C, RBO has a viscosity of 0.0398 Pa.s. It has an acid value of 1.2 [32]. Under MQL circumstances, pressurized air and a minuscule amount of coolant were delivered to the machining area. The coolant is supplied using a 3 mm nozzle with a flow rate of 70 ml/h and a minimal amount pressure of 5 bar.

For various cutting conditions, the irregularity of the machined face was measured using the SE-3500 roughness tester. Before starting the experiments, it was crucial to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the roughness measurements. To achieve this, the roughness tester was calibrated using slip gauges. The 3D roughness profiles of the milled surfaces were measured with the help of a non-contact Taylor Hobson profilometer. After each cutting trial, the cutting tool insert was carefully removed from the machine. Using the 2010F video measurement device, the tool wear was examined in detail. This instrument's high resolution of 0.5 µm, magnification of 0.7X–4.5X, and advanced image analysis software allow for accurate and repeatable measurements of the wear dimensions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CND based NPs in base oil
ce:section.ce:para:
Metal cutting with NPs is a cutting-edge technology that includes the use of small, engineered particles at the nanoscale to enhance the cutting performance of tools and improve the overall machining process. These NPs are typically added to CFs, which are then applied during the metal cutting operation. NPs can be dispersed in traditional CFs, creating what is known as NFs. These fluids offer improved C/L, and chip evacuation during the cutting process. The presence of NPs in the fluid can curtail friction amid tool-W/p, leading to less wear on the tool and a longer tool life [33]. The mechanism of NPs amid the tool-W/p junction is presented in Fig. 6 . Benefits of metal cutting with NPs may include:
•
Increased cutting speeds: NPs can help dissipate heat more effectively, allowing for higher cutting speeds without compromising the tool's integrity.
•
Extended tool life: The use of NPs can reduce tool wear and prolong tool life, resulting in cost savings and reduced downtime for tool changes.
•
Improved surface finish: The reduced friction and heat generation during cutting can lead to improved surface trait of the machined parts.
•
Enhanced efficiency: Metal cutting with NPs can lead to higher material removal rates and reduced energy consumption.

Rolling Effect of Nanoparticles: When NPs are introduced into lubricants, they can exhibit a rolling effect on tool-W/p surfaces. The NPs act as tiny spheres that can roll amid the moving parts, reducing direct surface-to-surface contact and friction. This rolling effect leads to lower frictional forces and less wear on the contacting surfaces, resulting in improved lubrication and enhanced mechanical efficiency.

Tribo-Film Formation with Nanoparticles: When NPs are present in lubricants, they can form a thin protective film, known as a tribo-film, on the contacting surfaces. These NPs generate a boundary layer that protects against wear and surface degradation through localized deformation and adhesion during sliding or rubbing. Tribo-films minimize friction and metal-to-metal contact, extending part life and performance.

Self-Repairing Effect of Nanoparticles: NPs can possess unique properties that enable them to facilitate self-repair of minor surface damage during sliding. When NPs present in lubricants, they can be released and fill in the small cracks or scratches that may form during cutting operation. This self-repairing effect helps to maintain the integrity of the surfaces and extends the overall lifespan of the parts.

Polishing Effect of Nanoparticles: In some applications, NPs can act as polishing agents during sliding or rubbing. The presence of NPs in lubricants can assist in the removal of rough surface imperfections, resulting in smoother and more polished surfaces. This polishing effect improves the surface characteristics of the component, reduces friction, and enhances the efficiency of the system.

The wettability of a liquid determines its ability to spread across a solid surface [33]. The characteristics of the liquid media, the substrate's temperature, and the surface texture, all have an impact on a liquid's wettability characteristic. The lesser heat transfer efficacy and decreased fluidity were caused by the increased contact angle [34]. The wettability of the RBO with diverse wt.% of NPs is presented in Fig. 7 . Later, when the droplet was stable on the substrate, the contact angle measurement was performed. The RBO without the inclusion of NPs was seen to have a larger contact angle of 39.12°. Nevertheless, as seen in Fig. 8 , after adding CND, the contact angle of RBO is decreased. The contact angle lessened from 30.29° to 21.93° as NPs concentration was raised. Reduced contact angle encourages wetting, which enhances the liquid's ability to interact, penetrate, and spread over the tool-chip-W/p junction. By adding NPs to the base fluid at a particular concentration, the contact angle may be reduced. After surpassing 1.0 wt%, NPs began to lose their dynamic stability, clustering instead of being evenly dispersed in the base fluid and finally settles down. Because of their greater density and weight, NPs can exert considerable downward pressure on oil molecules at the contact surface. This NPs clustering raised the contact angle, which decreased the wettability of the NF. The lubricating coating on the W/p’s surface may be degraded as a result of the NPs propensity to sink to the bottom, which might raise friction, produce a worse surface, and raise the cutting temperature when the w/p is machined.

Dynamic Viscosity: The key thermo-physical characteristic for figuring out CF deformation under shear stress is dynamic viscosity. It determines whether the fluid is distorted by extensional stress or shear stress and evaluates the fluid's internal resistance to flow. This characteristic has a substantial impact on the coefficient of convective heat transfer, affecting fluid flow and heat transmission [35]. The increase in dynamic viscosity (
μ
NF
)
of the NPs in RBO as a function of NP concentration (
∅ )
can be described using the Einstein's equation for dilute suspensions:
μ
NF
=
μ
base
×
( 1 + 2.5 × ∅ + 5.5 ×
∅
2
)
Where,
μ
base
is the dynamic viscosity of the RBO. The correlation amid the increasing NPs wt.%and changes in the dynamic viscosity of NFs is displayedin Fig. 9 . The graph unequivocally demonstrates that NPs infused into RBOwith increasing concentrations have a higher viscosity at room temperature. However, the dynamic viscosity of NF rises as CND concentration rises until it approaches a saturation threshold. This increment in viscosity wasdue to the inclusion of CND. As already established, too many NPsin CF causes inefficient dispersion and cluster formation. Therefore, higher NPs concentrations are not desired.

Thermal conductivity: Thermal conductivity measurement of different NF (RBO + CND) concentrations provide valuable insights into how the presence of NPs affects the fluid's ability to conduct heat. High thermal conductivity in a NF is desirable because it enhances the fluid's heat transfer efficiency. When NPs are dispersed in the RBO, they create additional pathways for heat transfer. The NPs can conduct heat more effectively than the base fluid alone, leading to an overall increase in thermal conductivity. The increase in thermal conductivity
( K
NF
)
of the NPs in RBO as a function of NP concentration
( ∅ )
can be described using a modified Maxwell's equation:
K
NF
=
K
base
×
( 1 + 2.5 × ∅ )
Where,
K
base
is the thermal conductivity of the RBO. At low NP concentrations, when the particles are well-dispersed in the base fluid, the increase in thermal conductivity is expected due to the effective thermal pathways provided by the NPs. Thermal conductivity first increased to 1 wt.% of CND concentration; however, as CND concentration increased further, conductivity dropped. Fig. 10 explains the disparity of conductivity regarding wt.% of CND + RBO. The thermal conductivity of a NF may decrease after reaching a certain concentration of NPs due to several factors. As mentioned earlier, increased NP concentration can lead to agglomeration [36]. Agglomerated NPs may act as insulating barriers, hindering the effective transfer of heat between particles and reducing the overall thermal conductivity. At high concentration, NPs might experience greater resistance to movement in the base fluid due to increased interactions amid particles. Hindered particle movement and reduced dispersion can limit the overall thermal conductivity enhancement [37].

Because it is most often used in manufacturing sectors worldwide, Ra was used in the current study to reflect the surface quality of the milled faces. Fig. 11 displays the Ra values of machined faces at distinct Vcs for dry, flood, MQL, and nano-MQL (N-MQL) machining circumstances, where it can be shown that Ra decreases with a rise in Vc. This effect is caused by a risein cutting temperatures as Vc increases, which encourages thermal softening of the W/pand more elimination of surface discontinuities and defects at high Vc, resulting in a lower Ra. The Ra value of 0.75 µm for the N-MQL condition, 0.99 µm for the MQL condition, 1.29 µm for the flood condition and 1.96 µm for the dry condition was observed at the Vc of 60 m/min and afr of 0.12 mm/rev. In N-MQL machining, 24 %, 42 % and 62 % improvement of surface finish was observed over the MQL, flood and dry conditions, respectively. These results allowto conclude that MQL and N-MQL strategies work well on Ra values and in contrast with dry and flood processing scenarios. There are two important settings in which this phenomenon can be studied. Two of these factors are the nozzle's orientation in the cutting area and the favorable characteristics of N-MQL. The MQL technique uses pressurized air to cool the cutter and W/p, removes chips to reduce heat in the cutting region, and reduces friction at the cutting tool-chip contact to increase tool life and surface quality. On the other hand, MQL and N-MQL are two promising strategies for reducing both fluid usage and manufacturing costs. The heat engendered at the cutting spot is dissipated more quickly, thanks to the immense thermal conductivity of NF. The tribological characteristics of NPs, which guarantee a high L/C ratio, can also improve the Ra at the cutting point. As reported by Anandan et al. [24], the NPs in MQL system produces a superior surface because it lubricates and cools the cutting area more effectively.

Images of the machined surface’s 3D surface morphology under diverse conditions at a Vc of 60 m/min and a fr of 0.12 mm/rev are exhibited in Fig. 12 . Although average Ra is a common approach for determining surface quality, it is understood that mapping a machined surface would be more beneficial owing to product pairs across the whole surface [38]. The existence of parallel lines formed by the feed motion of the cutter and the rotating motion of the tool resulted in the feed markings, which were predicted flaws on a machined surface. The distance amid the feed marks is associated with the magnitude of the fr. The magnitude of the fr is correlated with the separation between the two feed markings. It can be noticed that evenly spaced feed markings were seen under the N-MQL cutting strategy in contrast to other cooling conditions utilized in this investigation. This is because of the lubrication effect of RBO and the cooling nature of the CND present in the NF.

Cutting heat, surface morphology, and other outcomes connected to the cutting process are all directly affected by the cutter profile, tool material, machining inputs, tool coating, and lubrication environment [39]. The impact of the lubricating medium under various speed-feed combinations and constant DOC was examined in this section. Tool wear varies with lubrication media, Vc, and fr are seen in Fig. 13 . When it comes to LPBF-316L SS, N-MQL looks to be the most effective. The Vb is lessened by 68–76 %, 56–65 %, and 47–56 % with the application of N-MQL, MQL, and flood in contrast with a dry medium. The cutting edge of the tool becomes more brittle as an outcome of excessive heat generated in the cutting region, which, when paired with rise in Vc, leads to an acceleration in the rate at which the tool wears out. In the instance of MQL (RBO), it seems that the Vb depth is smaller. This is because RBO has higher tribological qualities, which results in less friction and hence generates less heat [20]. There are two distinct phenomena that can be seen in N-MQL. To begin with, VO produces a lubricating film on the cutting area, reducing the amount of friction present. Second, the incorporation of NPs into the RBO helps to lower the cutting heat at the too-W/p junction. Fig. 14 presents the crater side wear types under distinct cutting strategies. Wear phenomena play a crucial role in the performance and reliability of mechanical components and tools. These phenomena occur due to the interaction of surfaces under various operating conditions. Four common wear mechanisms are abrasion, adhesion, chipping, and coating peel-off [40]. The wear behavior can be significantly affected by the lubrication methods used during the machining process, such as dry, flood, MQL, and N-MQL. In dry conditions, without any lubrication, there is no protective film to reduce the abrasive action, resulting in more severe abrasion wear [38]. Under flood lubrication, a large amount of CF is applied to the tool-W/p junction, which helps to reduce abrasion wear significantly. The coolant acts as a barrier between the mating surfaces and prevents direct contact, reducing the mechanical interaction between the surfaces. In MQL, a tiny amount of lubricant is supplied straight to the cutting area. While MQL can provide some reduction in abrasion wear compared to dry and flood conditions. N-MQL is an advanced lubrication technique that uses NFs, which are suspensions of NPs in a base lubricant. N-MQL provides superior lubrication due to the unique properties of NPs [41]. They form a more effective barrier, reducing abrasive wear more efficiently. Adhesive wear occurs when two surfaces adhere and experience localized bonding due to molecular forces. In dry conditions, without lubrication, adhesion wear is more pronounced as there is no separating fluid to prevent direct contact and minimize bonding between surfaces. In flood lubrication, the lubricant film on the surfaces acts as a cushion, lessening the occurrence of direct contact and adhesive bonding. This significantly decreases adhesion wear. MQL and N-MQL can also help to reduce adhesion wear, as the presence of lubricant at the contact junction creates a thin layer, limiting direct contact and adhesion between the surfaces, the same was concluded by Ross et al [42]. Chipping wear happens when fragments of tool material are removed from the surface due to high-stress impact or localized loading [43]. This phenomenon is common in cutting operations where a cutting tool engages with the W/p. In dry condition, the absence of any lubrication exacerbates the chipping wear. In flood lubrication, the action of the fluid helps to dissipate heat and reduce the severity of chipping. MQL and N-MQL can also provide some level of protection against chipping wear, as the lubricant reduces the friction and heat generated during the cutting process, limiting the potential for material fracture and chipping. Coating peel-off occurs when a protective coating applied to the surface starts to degrade and detach from the substrate due to mechanical stresses or adhesion failure. In dry conditions, there was no lubricant to provide a buffer between the coating and the mating surface, leading to a higher risk of coating peel-off. Flood lubrication can help reduce the occurrence of coating peel-off by providing lubrication. MQL and N-MQL may also offer some advantages in terms of reducing coating peel-off. The lubricant, especially NF, can enhance the adhesion of the protective coating to the substrate, making it less prone to peel-off.

With variable concentrations, CND + RBO was prepared and defined. The contact angle, thermal conductivity, and dynamic viscosity of the prepared NFs were used to find the ideal concentration. Experiments on milling were carried out under 1 wt.% CND + RBO, MQL, flood and dry. The investigation's goal was to improve the surface features of LPBF-316L SS. The following are the outcomes:
•
Pure RBO has a contact angle of 39.12°, according to the wettability examination, the contact angle progressively lowers as CND is deposited. At 1 wt.% CND concentration, the lowest contact angle value was obtained as a reason of even distribution in the base fluid.
•
The thermal conductivity of RBO is significantly raised by the addition of NPs. The thermal conductivity value at the higher concentration (1 wt.%) in this investigation was 0.29 W/mK. The same pattern was observed in dynamic viscosity with the highest value under 1 wt.% concentration. A higher concentration of NPs enhanced the dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity of the NF, at the same time clustering of NPs decreased the thermo-physical properties
•
Under N-MQL, the Ra was less for the entire speed-feed combo. In relation to the N-MQL condition, the Ra was reduced to 60–63 %, 40–43 %, and 22–25 % for dry, flood, and MQL, respectively. The 3D surface topography assessments conducted for the N-MQL cutting approach showed a flat surface with fewer lows and peaks. This is because, at the tool-W/p intersection, N-MQL offers both L/C operations.
•
In comparison to the other cutting procedures investigated, the dry cutting environment has the highest Vb. When the N-MQL was used during the milling operation, the Vb decreased. The Vb is lessened by 68–76 %, 56–65 %, and 47–56 % with the application of N-MQL, MQL, and flood in contrast with a dry medium. Due to the cutting region's excellent heat dissipation under N-MQL condition, the heat and friction at the tool-W/p junction were greatly decreased, which led to quick tool wear mitigation.
•
Abrasion, adhesion, coating peel-off, and chipping were seen on the crater side of the tool. The proficiency of the RBO + CND provides a C/L effect at the cutting area curtails the wear mechanisms on the crater side and helps to extend the tool life span.

Nimel Sworna Ross: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigations, Validation, Writing. Peter Madindwa Mashinini: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding. Ritu Rai: Analysis, Writing, Editing. Munish Kumar Gupta: Analysis, Writing, Editing

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
